hook: Britain's oldest surviving Indian restaurant, serving curry above Regent Street since 1926
facts: Opened 1926 by Edward Palmer, retired Anglo-Indian army officer
Diners reportedly included Nehru, Indira Gandhi, Chaplin and Churchill
Won a Michelin star in 2016
Reached its 100th year in 2026
w: Britain's oldest surviving Indian restaurant opened in 1926, the venture of a retired Anglo-Indian army officer who brought the Raj club dining room to Regent Street. Nehru, Indira Gandhi and Chaplin supposedly ate here; the spices still arrive by the sackful. A century up the stairs.

hook: Sizzling Punjabi grill house in Whitechapel, family-run since 1972 and famous for the queue
facts: Founded 1972 by Mohammed Tayyab, now run by his three sons
Grew from a small cafe to a three-floor restaurant
Sizzling lamb chops introduced around 1980
Bring-your-own-bottle policy
w: Mohammed Tayyab opened a tiny cafe off Whitechapel Road in 1972; his sons turned it into three sizzling floors of Punjabi grill. Lamb chops arrive spitting on hot iron, you bring your own beer, and the queue out the door became the most famous in the East End. Chaos, marinated.

hook: Whitechapel Pakistani institution grilling tandoori meat and ghee-rich curry since 1972
facts: Established 1972, grown from a small diner to a large multi-floor restaurant
Family-run, known for authentic Pakistani Punjabi cooking
Famous for grilled lamb chops and karahi
Weekend queues out the door
w: From a small Umberston Street diner in 1972 to a cavernous canteen seating hundreds, this place built its name on ghee-laden karahi and lamb chops charred over fierce heat. Diners travel across the city and queue at weekends for Punjabi cooking with no airs. Bring an appetite and a bottle.

hook: Theatreland's red-fronted seafood warren, serving oysters off St Martin's Court since 1896
facts: Founded 1896 by oyster trader Josef Sheekey
Lord Salisbury granted permission for post-theatre supper parties
Four interconnecting rooms hung with portraits of actors
Marked its 130th year in 2026
w: In 1896 Lord Salisbury let an oyster man named Josef Sheekey serve fish in St Martin's Court, on condition he cater the PM's post-theatre suppers. The red frontage and warren of little rooms still pull in actors after curtain-down, walls thick with their photographs. Theatreland's fish counter.

hook: French bistro hidden in a cobbled yard tangled in a gruesome murder legend
facts: Set in cobbled Bleeding Heart Yard off Greville Street
Tavern on the site dates to 1746
Bleeding Heart Yard appears in Dickens's Little Dorrit
Restaurant, bistro and tavern around the yard
legend: Legend says Lady Elizabeth Hatton was found torn limb from limb here, heart still pumping blood onto the cobbles
w: French cooking served around a cobbled courtyard with a grisly name. Legend says Lady Elizabeth Hatton was found here torn limb from limb, her heart still pumping blood onto the stones; the dull truth says she died in bed. Dickens put the Plornishes in this yard. Steak frites, with folklore.

hook: London's oldest French restaurant, named for the snails farmed in its Soho basement
facts: Founder Georges Gaudin first served snails in London in the 1890s
Moved to 48 Greek Street in 1927 and took the snail's name
Ran a snail farm in the basement
Housed in a Georgian townhouse dating from 1741
w: Georges Gaudin became the first man to serve snails in England, then in 1927 moved to Greek Street and let customers rename the place after his signature dish. He ran a snail farm in the basement, a talking point for Soho. Coco Chanel and de Gaulle ate here. The garlic butter endures.

hook: The original theatreland canteen, an unlicensed cafe in 1917 that became London's starriest table
facts: Opened 1917 by Abel Giandolini as an unlicensed Italian cafe
Started with lino floors, paper napkins and no drinks licence
Sent meals to actors' dressing rooms opposite the theatres
Expanded to the present West Street premises in 1929
legend: Legend says the name came from actress Alice Delysia's line, 'we will cling together like the ivy'
w: It began in 1917 as a lino-floored cafe with no drinks licence, the waitresses nipping to the pub for a customer's wine. Sending hot meals to actors across the road built its theatrical fame. Legend says the name came from a star's line: we will cling together like the ivy. So it did.

hook: Marble-topped oyster bar off Piccadilly, shucking on the same spot since 1916
facts: Opened 1916 by William 'Bill' Bentley
Bentley invested in oyster beds at West Mersea
Original marble-topped bar still in use
One of London's oldest oyster bars
w: Bill Bentley sold oysters from a barrow by Clacton pier, bought his own beds at West Mersea, then in 1916 opened this oyster bar down a Mayfair side street. The marble counter where shuckers have worked for over a century stands unchanged. Order a dozen native, watch the knife. Briny ritual.

hook: Cavernous art-deco supper room that fed Mrs Simpson, the Prince of Wales and bright young things
facts: Founded 1929 by Giovanni Quaglino
Patrons included Edward, Prince of Wales and Mrs Simpson
Closed 1977, revived by Terence Conran in 1993
Reached by a sweeping staircase into a vast basement room
w: Giovanni Quaglino opened this glittering basement in 1929; the Mountbattens, Evelyn Waugh and the Prince of Wales with Mrs Simpson swept down its staircase. Shuttered in 1977, Conran resurrected it in 1993 as the loudest table in town. Make an entrance, then order the seafood platter. Pure deco.

hook: Mount Street oyster house, founded 1851, where Ian Fleming took his martini shaken not stirred
facts: Founded 1851 by fishmonger John Scott as an oyster warehouse
Sat on Coventry Street until moving to Mount Street in 1967
Ian Fleming a regular in the 1950s
Among London's grandest seafood rooms
legend: Reportedly the spot where Fleming settled on James Bond's 'shaken not stirred' martini
w: An 1851 oyster warehouse turned Mayfair grandee, draped in green and crowded with art. Ian Fleming dined here through the 1950s, and the place claims his James Bond martini, shaken not stirred, was settled at its bar. The crushed-ice counter still gleams. Old money, on the half shell.

hook: Grand 1982 Indian dining room that dragged the curry house upmarket into the Raj revival
facts: Opened 1982 in South Kensington
Pioneered modern regional Indian cooking in a European brasserie format
Set in a conservatory-fronted room near Gloucester Road
Long a benchmark for upscale Indian dining
w: When it opened in 1982, the Raj-revival palm court and white-linen ambition rewrote what an Indian restaurant could be in London, marrying regional home cooking to brasserie polish. The conservatory dining room still feels like a colonial fever dream. Where the curry house grew up. Order the dhansak.

hook: London's oldest Swiss restaurant, melting fondue on Wardour Street since 1974
facts: Opened 1974, the capital's oldest Swiss restaurant
Owner Armin Loetscher claims to sell at least 100 fondues a day
Menu runs to raclette, Berner Platte and veal dishes
Alpine chalet decor in the heart of Soho
w: Cowbells, pine panelling and bubbling cheese on Wardour Street since 1974, the capital's oldest Swiss restaurant and a fondue stronghold five decades deep. The owner reckons he shifts a hundred pots a day. Spear bread, twirl, lose your fork to a forfeit. Alpine kitsch, defiantly Soho.

hook: Marylebone Lane chippy frying cod in the same room since 1914
facts: Frying on Marylebone Lane since 1914
Original cast-iron art nouveau range kept in the room
Unlicensed, with a bring-your-own policy
A Marylebone Village institution
w: Cod and chips from the same Marylebone Lane room since 1914, with an ornate old cast-iron frying range still standing in the corner like a museum piece. No licence, so bring your own bottle. Battered haddock, mushy peas, formica calm in a postcode that forgot to gentrify it. A proper chippy.

hook: A greasy-spoon shrine to Princess Diana, wallpapered with her photographs near Kensington Palace
facts: Opened 1989, walls covered in Princess Diana photographs
Diana, who lived nearby, reportedly visited
Owner displays a signed photo gifted by her in 1989
Steps from Kensington Palace Gardens
w: Every inch of wall by Kensington Palace papered with photographs of Princess Diana, who lived round the corner and reportedly dropped in. The owner treasures a signed picture she gave him in 1989. Part cafe, part roadside shrine, serving fry-ups and mezze under her gaze. Devotion with chips.

hook: Slender Charlotte Street fine-dining room that has held a Michelin star since the 1990s
facts: Opened December 1991 by David Moore and Richard Neat
Awarded a Michelin star in 1996
A narrow Fitzrovia townhouse dining room
Among London's most enduring fine-dining survivors
w: A narrow Charlotte Street townhouse that quietly became one of London's longest-running fine-dining survivors, open since 1991 and Michelin-starred since 1996. It launched young chefs who went on to fame while the room itself stayed small, serious and discreet. Fitzrovia's stubborn temple of the tasting menu.

hook: London's oldest family-run French restaurant, with a zinc bar salvaged from a Lyon brothel
facts: Founded 1943 by Jean Viala, London's oldest family-run French restaurant
Head waiter Alain Lhermitte took over in 1972 and expanded it
Zinc bar reputedly came from a brothel in Lyon
Now run by its third successive family
w: London's oldest family-run French restaurant, founded in 1943 and grown from one room to four, the head waiter buying it from his boss in 1972. The zinc bar reputedly came from a Lyon brothel; the original dining room hardly changed. Snails, steak and Gallic clutter off Seven Dials. Vieux Paris.
